movie.short_description: Participants passively watch a 30-second movie clip (nature, animated romance, or landscape scenery) with no audio and no subtitles.
movie.detailed_description: Participants passively watch a single 30-second video clip per block, presented without audio or subtitles, and are instructed to keep their head still and attend to the screen; no response is required. Three clip conditions are available: 'nature' — a nature documentary clip (kickboxing kangaroos, from 'Planet Earth II: Islands'); 'romance' — an emotional love story between two characters from the Pixar movie 'Up'; and 'landscape' — an aesthetically-pleasing clip depicting diverse scenery (sourced from Vimeo). The clips of a given condition are presented in order across runs so they unfold as a continuous sequence over the session. Movie watching is a naturalistic-viewing task that engages visual and dynamic-scene processing, with the romance condition additionally recruiting social and emotional processing.
reference: Nguyen, V. T., Sonkusare, S., Stadler, J., Hu, X., Breakspear, M., & Guo, C. C. (2017). Distinct cerebellar contributions to cognitive-perceptual dynamics during natural viewing. Cerebral Cortex, 27(12), 5652-5662.

King, M., Hernandez-Castillo, C. R., Poldrack, R. A., Ivry, R. B., & Diedrichsen, J. (2019). Functional boundaries in the human cerebellum revealed by a multi-domain task battery. Nature neuroscience, 22(8), 1371-1378.

run_number.description: Run number, used to select which clip to show from the stimulus list.

trial_dur.description: Duration of each trial (clip) in seconds.

condition.description: Which condition to use: 'romance', 'nature', or 'landscape'. If None, all non-practice clips are used.

oddball.short_description: Participants see a black or red 'K' or 'O' on screen and press a button only when a red 'K' appears.
oddball.detailed_description: Participants see a single letter ('K' or 'O') briefly shown in either black or red on each trial. They press a button when they see a red 'K' (the infrequent target) and withhold responses to all other stimuli. The red 'K' appears on 10% of the trials, with the remaining trials split between black 'K' (40%), black 'O' (40%), and red 'O' (10%) non-targets. This task explores detection of transient responses to salient, visual oddball targets that are uncommon relative to irrelevant nontargets and distracting nontargets. The goal of the task is to activate the SAL/PMN and CG-OP networks.

trial_dur.description: Duration the stimulus is displayed in seconds.

oddball.reference: Du, J., DiNicola, L. M., Angeli, P. A., Saadon-Grosman, N., Sun, W., Kaiser, S., ... & Buckner, R. L. (2024). Organization of the human cerebral cortex estimated within individuals: networks, global topography, and function. Journal of neurophysiology, 131(6), 1014-1082.

demand_grid.short_description: Participants see a sequence of steps that each light up boxes in a grid, mentally combine them into a single pattern, then choose which of two grids matches the full sequence.
demand_grid.detailed_description: Paritipants see a sequence of steps, where each step lights up a set of boxes in a grid, and add each step to a running representation of the overall pattern. After the sequence, two grids appear side by side, one showing the correct pattern and the other with one of the steps modified. Participants need to make a button press to choose the correct options. Grid size, number of steps, and boxes lit per step are adjustable to vary working memory load. Contrasting harder against easier trials localizes the domain-general Multiple Demand (MD) network in frontal and parietal cortex. The easier>harder or easier>fixation contrasts can be used to localize the Default Mode Network (DMN).
demand_grid.reference: Fedorenko, E., Duncan, J., & Kanwisher, N. (2013). Broad domain generality in focal regions of frontal and parietal cortex. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America, 110(41), 16616-16621.

responses.description: Response keys for left and right choices.

num_steps.description: Number of steps in the sequence shown to the participant.

num_boxes_lit.description: Number of boxes lit up per step.

trial_dur.description: Duration of each trial in seconds.

question_dur.description: Duration of the question phase where the two options are shown.

sequence_dur.description: Duration of the sequence presentation phase.

action_observation.short_description: Participants watch a video of a  knot being tied, followed by a rotating 360 view of the knot. A made-up name of the knot is displayed.
action_observation.detailed_description: Participants watch videos of a knot being tied then immediately a 360-degree view of the completed knot. Each video/trial is 14s long. The first trial in a block shows the knot-tying action, and subsequent trial shows the static knot view. No motor response during scanning is required, but participants are instructed to learn the knot so they could reproduce it after the scan. The task is designed to engage action observation.
action_observation.reference: Cross, E. S., Cohen, N. R., Hamilton, A. F. de C., Ramsey, R., Wolford, G., & Grafton, S. T. (2012). Physical experience leads to enhanced object perception in parietal cortex: Insights from knot tying. Neuropsychologia, 50(14), 3207-3217.

run_number.description: Run number, used to select which knot stimulus to show.

trial_dur.description: Duration of each video trial in seconds.

visual_search.short_description: A set of letter Ls with different orientations are presented each trial, participants must identify if 'L' is present or not.
visual_search.reference: Duncan, J., & Humphreys, G. W. (1989). Visual search and stimulus similarity. Psychological review, 96(3), 433.
visual_search.detailed_description: Participants view a display of geometric shapes at different rotations (90, 180, 270, 360 degrees) and must determine whether a L is present in the canonical orientation. The number of shapes is flexible, on easy trials 4 shapes are shown, on hard trials 8 shapes are shown. Participants press one button if the target is present and another if it is absent. The task requires a serial search of the display as indicated by RTs increasing linearly in the array size. The task requires shifts of visual attention and elicits eye-movement related activity.

responses.description: Response keys for target present and absent.

trial_dur.description: Duration of each trial in seconds.

easy_prob.description: Probability of an easy trial (4 stimuli vs. 8).

serial_reaction_time.detailed_description: Four boxes are displayed in a row. On each trial one box lights up green and the participant presses the corresponding button as fast as possible. Stimuli are randomized with the constraint that the same box never lights up on consecutive trials. The task measures basic visuomotor response speed.
serial_reaction_time.reference: Nissen, M. J., & Bullemer, P. (1987). Attentional requirements of learning: Evidence from performance measures. Cognitive psychology, 19(1), 1-32.

responses.description: Response keys mapped to each box position.

initial_wait.description: Wait time in seconds before the first stimulus, showing empty boxes.

trial_dur.description: Duration the green box is displayed in seconds.

serial_reaction_time.short_description: Single boxes light up one at a time in a row of four. Press corrosponding button as fast as possible.

verb_generation.short_description: Participants see words one at a time and, either silently read each word (read condition) or covertly generate a verb associated with it (generate condition).
verb_generation.detailed_description: Participants see words presented one at a time. In the 'read' condition they silently read each word as it appears. In the 'generate' condition they covertly think of a verb associated with each word. No button response is required. The generate>read contrast is designed to engage language and semantic-retrieval processing.

condition.description: Which condition the block runs: 'read' (silently read each word) or 'generate' (covertly generate an associated verb).

trial_dur.description: Duration each word is displayed in seconds.

verb_generation.reference: Adapted by changing into covert rather than overt reading/generation from Raichle, M. E., Fiez, J. A., Videen, T. O., MacLeod, A. M. K., Pardo, J. V., Fox, P. T., & Petersen, S. E. (1994). Practice-related changes in human brain functional anatomy during nonmotor learning. Cerebral cortex, 4(1), 8-26.

rest.detailed_description: Participants lie still and fixate on a central crosshair. No stimuli are presented and no response is required. For most fMRI experiments, rest is used as the baseline condition, and it is recommended to include it into every task battery to allow for alignment with other datasets.
rest.short_description: Lie in scanner with a fixation cross.

theory_of_mind.short_description: Participants read a short story, followed by a statement they must judge as true or false.
theory_of_mind.reference: Saxe, R. and Kanwisher, N. (2003) People thinking about thinking people: The role of the temporo-parietal junction in “theory of mind”, NeuroImage, 19(4), pp. 1835-1842.
theory_of_mind.detailed_description: Participants read short stories. Each story is followed by a statement and a true/false judgment. In the belief condition, stories require inferring another person's mental state (false belief); in the photo condition, stories require reasoning about outdated conent of a physical or external representation such as a photograph, a map or a book, whose information no longer matches the current state of the world. Both conditions are matched in structure (each involves holding a mental represntation that conflicts with the current reality). Only the belief condition involves another person's mind. Participants press a button to indicate whether the statement about the story is true or false. Task is designed to engage mentalizing (theory of mind): reasoning about other people's mental states.

responses.description: Response keys for True and False.

run_number.description: Run number, used to select stimuli for that run.

trial_dur.description: Total duration of each trial in seconds.

story_dur.description: Duration the story is displayed in seconds.

question_dur.description: Duration the question is displayed in seconds.

condition.description: Which condition to use: belief or photo.

text_height.description: Height of the story and question text in degrees of visual angle.

n_back.short_description: 2AFC, is the current stimulus the same as the one presented n ago
n_back.detailed_description: A 2-back working memory task. Participants view a sequence of images and must indicate whether the current image matches the one shown two items ago. The task engages working memory networks.

responses.description: Response keys for match and no-match.

trial_dur.description: Duration each stimulus is displayed in seconds.

picture_scale.description: Scaling factor for stimulus images. Values > 1 enlarge the pictures.

stim.description: List of stimulus image filenames to draw from.

tongue_movement.short_description: Participants move their tongue right to left against the upper premolar teeth, paced by a flashing circle around a fixation cross.
tongue_movement.detailed_description: Participants move their tongue from right to left, touching their upper premolar teeth on each side. A flashing black circle around a fixation crosshair guides the rhythm at 1-second periods: a black circle cues a movement to the right, no circle cues a movement to the left. The task targets tongue-related somatomotor activation.
tongue_movement.reference: A Third Somatomotor Representation in the Human Cerebellum (Bucknet et al., 2022)

trial_dur.description: Duration of each tongue movement cycle in seconds.

auditory_narrative.short_description: Participants listen to 30 second snippets of a moth radiohour story (where there's smoke)
auditory_narrative.detailed_description: Participants listen to 30-second audio snippets from The Moth Radio Hour story 'Where There's Smoke'. The clips are presented sequentially across runs so the participant follows the narrative. The task is designed to engage auditory cortex, the language network and higher-level narrative comprehension.
auditory_narrative.reference: Tang, J., LeBel, A., Jain, S., & Huth, A. G. (2023). Semantic reconstruction of continuous language from non-invasive brain recordings. Nature Neuroscience, 26(5), 858-866.

run_number.description: Run number, used to select which audio clip to play.

trial_dur.description: Duration of each audio clip in seconds.

spatial_navigation.short_description: Imagining walking room to room in childhood home.
spatial_navigation.reference: Boly, M., Coleman, M. R., Davis, M. H., Hampshire, A., Bor, D., Moonen, G., ... & Owen, A. M. (2007). When thoughts become action: an fMRI paradigm to study volitional brain activity in non-communicative brain injured patients. Neuroimage, 36(3), 979-992.
spatial_navigation.detailed_description: Participants imagine walking from room to room in their childhood home. No visual stimuli are presented beyond the instruction. The task is designed to engage spatial memory and mental navigation.

trial_dur.description: Duration of the imagination period in seconds.

passage_listening.short_description: Participants listen to intact or accoustically degraded (mufffled) speech passages
passage_listening.detailed_description: Participants listen to excerpts from speeches and talks from several sources (e.g., The moth podcast, Ted talks, celebrity interviews). In the intact codition the audio is intelligible. In the degraded condition the same type of audio is acoustically degraded so it is matched in lower level acoustic properties but the content can no longer be understood. Participants are instructed to only listen attentively; no response is required. The intact>degraded contrast localizes high-level language processing brain regions.
passage_listening.reference: Scott, T. L., Gallee, J., & Fedorenko, E. (2017). A new fun and robust version of an fMRI localizer for the frontotemporal language system. Cognitive Neuroscience, 8(3), 167-176.

condition.description: If set, only trials of this condition are included ('intact' or 'degraded').

run_number.description: Run number, used to select which passage to play.

trial_dur.description: Duration of each passage in seconds.

reading.short_description: Participants read sentences or nonword sequences.
reading.reference: Fedorenko, E., Hsieh, P.-J., Nieto-Castanon, A., Whitfield-Gabrieli, S., & Kanwisher, N. (2010). New method for fMRI investigations of language: defining ROIs functionally in individual subjects. Journal of Neurophysiology, 104(2), 1177-1194.
reading.detailed_description: Participants read sentences or nonword sequences, where words/nonwords are shown one at a time. Participants are instructed to read silently as they would when reading a book. At the end of each sentence/nonword sequence, participants see a picture of a finger pressing a button; whenever they see that picture they need to make a button press (to help them stay alert during throughout the task. The sentences>nonwords contrast can be used to localize high-level language processing brain regions,  i.e., regions that support lexico-semantic and combinatorial (syntactic and semantic) processes

condition.description: If set, only trials of this condition are included ('sentences' or 'nonwords').

run_number.description: Run number, used to select which sentences to show.

trial_dur.description: Duration of each sentence presentation in seconds.

finger_sequence.detailed_description: A sequence of 6 digits (1-4) is displayed and participants press the corresponding buttons in order as quickly and accurately as possible. No consecutive repeats appear in the sequence.

responses.description: Response keys mapped to each finger.

trial_dur.description: Duration of each trial in seconds.

flexion_extension.detailed_description: Participants alternate between flexing and extending their toes, guided by visual cues.

trial_dur.description: Duration of the flexion/extension period in seconds.

stim_dur.description: Duration of each flex/extend cue in seconds.

semantic_prediction.short_description: Sentence presented, participants decide if last word makes sense.
semantic_prediction.detailed_description: On each trial a sentence stem is displayed and participants must decide whether the final word makes the sentence meaningful or meaningless. The task engages language comprehension and semantic processing regions.

run_number.description: Run number, used to select which sentences to present.

trial_dur.description: Duration of each trial in seconds.

sentence_dur.description: Duration the sentence stem is displayed before the final word appears, in seconds.

rmet.short_description: Participants see pictures of faces with different emotions presented (eyes only) along with four options (for either emotion or age), participants must identify which option describes person best.
rmet.detailed_description: Participants see a photograph of a person's eye region (just the eyes) together with four descriptive words, and press a button to choose the option that best fits the person. In the emotion condition the options are mental-state words and participants infer what the person is thinking or feeling, which engages mentalizing (theory of mind) and social cognition. In the age condition the same eye images are shown but the options are age ranges and participants judge the person's age instead; this is matched in low-level visual demands and face processing but does not require inferring a mental state, so it serves as a control. The emotion>age contrast isolates mental-state inference from basic face perception.
rmet.reference: Kim, H.A. et al. (2024) 'Multiracial Reading the Mind in the Eyes Test (MRMET): An inclusive version of an influential measure', Behavior Research Methods. Revised from Baron-Cohen, Wheelwright, Hill, Raste, & Plumb, 2001

responses.description: Response keys mapped to the four options.

run_number.description: Run number, used to select which stimuli to present.

trial_dur.description: Duration each stimulus is displayed in seconds.

option_text_height.description: Height of the answer-option text in degrees of visual angle.

option_position_scale.description: Spatial scaling factor for option positions. Values < 1 bring options closer together.

condition.description: If set, only trials of this condition are included ('emotion' or 'age').

picture_sequence.short_description: 4 cartoon pictures are presented, participants must order them chronologically.
picture_sequence.reference: Heleven, E., van Dun, K. and Van Overwalle, F. (2019) ‘The posterior Cerebellum is involved in constructing Social Action Sequences: An fMRI Study’, Scientific Reports, 9(1), pp. 1–11. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-46962-7.

story_sequence.short_description: 4 sentences are presented, participants must order them chronologically.
story_sequence.reference: Heleven, E., van Dun, K. and Van Overwalle, F. (2019) ‘The posterior Cerebellum is involved in constructing Social Action Sequences: An fMRI Study’, Scientific Reports, 9(1), pp. 1–11. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-46962-7.

action_prediction.short_description: An action video is shown of an actor is either taking a penalty shot or greeting a person. The video stops right before a contact with the ball or with the other person occurs.  Participants must predict if the ball lands left or right or if the person greets with a handshake or hug.
action_prediction.detailed_description: The stopping of the video requires participants to interpret relatively subtle signs that reveal the intention of the actor. The soccer condition is designed to engage general action prediction, whereas the greeting condition is designed to engage prediction in a social context.
action_prediction.reference: King, M. (2022). Investigating cortico-cerebellar contributions to cognition. UC Berkeley. ProQuest ID: King_berkeley_0028E_21350. Merritt ID: ark:/13030/m5wb6kvd. Retrieved from https://escholarship.org/uc/item/61w6t6sk

responses.description: Response keys for the two prediction options.

run_number.description: Run number, used to select which stimuli to present.

trial_dur.description: Duration of the video presentation in seconds.

question_dur.description: Duration the prediction question is displayed in seconds.

condition.description: If set, only trials of this condition are included (e.g. 'soccer_easy', 'greeting_hard').

strange_stories.short_description: Clips of relationship stories, participants are asked to answer questions about the interaction.
strange_stories.reference: Murray, K. et al. (2017). A new test of advanced theory of mind: The “Strange Stories Film Task” captures social processing differences in adults with autism spectrum disorders, Autism Research, 10(6), pp. 1120-1132. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1002/aur.1744.

faux_pas.short_description: Stories of (potential) faux pas situations, participants must answer questions about the interaction.
faux_pas.reference: Stone, V.E., Baron-Cohen, S. & Knight, R.T. (1998). Frontal lobe contributions to theory of mind. Journal of Cognitive Neuroscience, 10, 640-656. Gregory, C. , Lough, S., Stone, V.E., Erzinclioglu, S., Martin, L., Baron-Cohen, S. & Hodges, J. (2002). Theory of mind in frontotemporal dementia and Alzheimer's disease: Theoretical and practical implications. Brain, 125, 752-64.
faux_pas.detailed_description: On each trial a short social scenario is presented followed by a yes/no question about whether anyone said something they should not have said. The task is designed to engage social cognition and mentalizing regions.

run_number.description: Run number, used to select which stories to present.

trial_dur.description: Total duration of each trial in seconds.

story_dur.description: Duration the story text is displayed in seconds.

question1_dur.description: Duration the faux-pas detection question is displayed in seconds.

text_height.description: Height of the story and question text in degrees of visual angle.

condition.description: If set, only trials of this condition are included ('social' or 'control').

frith_happe.short_description: Video clips of triangle animations, participants must determine whether interaction was random, goal-directed or a mentalizing interaction.
frith_happe.reference: Livingston, L.A. et al. (2021). Further developing the Frith-Happé animations: A quicker, more objective, and web-based test of theory of mind for autistic and neurotypical adults. Autism Research, 14(9), pp. 1905-1912. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1002/aur.2575.

liking.short_description: Video clips of people interacting, participants must determine if the interaction was positive or negative.
liking.reference: Heerey, E.A. and Gilder, T.S.E. (2019). The subjective value of a smile alters social behaviour, PLoS ONE, 14(12), pp. 1-19. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0225284.

affective.short_description: Participants view a picture (scenes, animals, or foods) and choose whether it is pleasant or unpleasant.
affective.detailed_description: Participants view pictures drawn from the International Affective Picture System (IAPS) — depicting scenes, animals, and foods — and indicate via a button press whether each image is pleasant or unpleasant. The task is designed to engage affective/emotional valence processing.
reference: Lang, P. J., Bradley, M. M., & Cuthbert, B. N. (1997). International affective picture system (IAPS): Technical manual and affective ratings. NIMH Center for the Study of Emotion and Attention, 1(39-58), 3.

Moulton, E. A., Elman, I., Pendse, G., Schmahmann, J., Becerra, L., & Borsook, D. (2011). Aversion-related circuitry in the cerebellum: responses to noxious heat and unpleasant images. Journal of neuroscience, 31(10), 3795-3804.

trial_dur.description: Duration each image is displayed in seconds.

finger_rhythmic.short_description: Rhythmic tapping production task. Participants are instructed to produce a regular series of taps at a prescribed target interval.
finger_rhythmic.reference: Ivry, R. B., & Keele, S. W. (1989). Timing Functions of the Cerebellum. Journal of Cognitive Neuroscience, 1(2), 136-152.
finger_rhythmic.detailed_description: A timing production task in which participants listen to a train of auditory tones and tap along at the same pace. The task engages motor timing.

trial_dur.description: Duration of each trial (includes start text, tone train, and buffer) in seconds.

ioi.description: Inter-onset interval(s) in seconds. A single value gives one pace; a list (e.g. [0.3, 0.6, 1.2]) creates trials at different paces, randomly assigned.

time_perception.short_description: Assess the precision of internal timing mechanisms by measuring participants' perceptual acuity in discriminating short auditory intervals. Participants are presented with sequences of tones and must decide whether a comparison interval is longer or shorter than a standard reference interval.
time_perception.reference: Ivry, R. B., & Keele, S. W. (1989). Timing Functions of the Cerebellum. Journal of Cognitive Neuroscience, 1(2), 136-152.

sensmot_control.short_description: Assess basic sensorimotor control. Measure reaction time and response accuracy to evaluate low-level perceptual and motor processing. Part 1 is a Go/No-Go. Part 2 is a 2AFC
sensmot_control.reference: Koechlin, E., & Summerfield, C. (2007). An information theoretical approach to prefrontal executive function. Trends in cognitive sciences, 11(6), 229-235.Koechlin 2007

semantic_switching.short_description: sentence presented, participants decide if last word makes sense.
